extra.message:
Hardcoded Database Credentials in Configuration File

The database username and password are hardcoded in plaintext in the configuration file (user: postgres, password: postgres). These are weak, default credentials stored in a file that is likely committed to version control. In a public-facing application handling PII, this directly exposes the database to unauthorized access.

Impact: An attacker who gains access to the source code repository or the configuration file can directly authenticate to the PostgreSQL database and access all PII and application data.

Fix: Use environment variables or a secrets management system (e.g., Docker secrets, HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) to inject credentials at runtime. Never commit credentials to version control.

CSRF Protection Disabled (Middleware Commented Out)

The CSRF middleware is commented out in the application middleware stack. All POST endpoints are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks. While CSRF tokens are generated in templates, they are never validated because the middleware is disabled.

Impact: Attackers can craft malicious pages that submit forged requests on behalf of authenticated users, including creating students, courses, evaluating students, and submitting reviews.

Fix: Uncomment the csrf_middleware in the middlewares list to enable CSRF protection.

Jinja2 autoescape=False enables XSS across all templates

The Jinja2 template engine is configured with autoescape=False, which means all template expressions ({{ }}) will render user-controlled data without HTML escaping. This is the root cause enabling stored and reflected XSS across every template in the application.

Impact: Attackers can inject arbitrary JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and actions on behalf of victims.

Fix: Set autoescape=True in the setup_jinja call to enable automatic HTML escaping for all template expressions.

SQL Injection via String Formatting in Student.create

The Student.create method constructs a SQL query using Python string formatting (% operator) with the 'name' parameter directly interpolated into the query string. The query is built as `"INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}` and then executed without parameterization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing a crafted 'name' value (e.g., `'); DROP TABLE students; --`).

Impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary SQL commands against the PostgreSQL database, potentially reading, modifying, or deleting any data including PII, user credentials, and admin accounts. Full database compromise is possible.

Fix: Use parameterized queries instead of string formatting. Replace with: `q = "INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%(name)s)"` and pass `{'name': name}` as the second argument to `cur.execute(q, {'name': name})`.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

The check_password method uses MD5 hashing (`md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()`) to verify passwords. MD5 is a fast, unsalted hash that is trivially brute-forced with modern hardware and rainbow tables. This is especially critical given the application stores PII.

Impact: If the database is compromised (e.g., via the SQL injection in Student.create), all user passwords can be recovered almost instantly using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force. This enables account takeover and credential stuffing attacks.

Fix: Use a proper password hashing algorithm designed for password storage, such as bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. These algorithms include salting and are computationally expensive to resist brute-force attacks. Example: `import bcrypt; bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode('utf-8'), self.pwd_hash.encode('utf-8'))`.

XSS via auth_user fields in base.jinja2

User first_name, middle_name, and last_name are rendered without escaping in the navigation bar with autoescape disabled. If user names contain malicious content (e.g., from database), this is exploitable.

Impact: XSS via user profile data rendered in the navigation bar on every page.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter or enable autoescape=True globally.

Stored XSS via course title in course.jinja2

The course title is rendered without escaping ({{ course.title }}) on the course detail page with autoescape disabled.

Impact: Stored XSS via malicious course title.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter or enable autoescape=True globally.

Stored XSS via course title in courses.jinja2

The course title is rendered without escaping ({{ course.title }}) with autoescape disabled. Course titles are user-supplied and stored in the database.

Impact: An attacker can inject JavaScript via a malicious course title that executes when any user views the courses list.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter or enable autoescape=True globally.

Stored XSS via student name in evaluate.jinja2

Student name is rendered without escaping ({{ student.name }}) in the aside section of evaluate.jinja2 with autoescape disabled. Note: line 9 uses |e but line 25 does not.

Impact: Stored XSS via malicious student name.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter: {{ student.name | e }}.

Stored XSS via course title in review.jinja2

Course title is rendered without escaping ({{ course.title }}) in the review form page with autoescape disabled.

Impact: Stored XSS via malicious course title.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter or enable autoescape=True globally.

Stored XSS via course title in student.jinja2

Course title is rendered without escaping ({{ course.title }}) on the student detail page with autoescape disabled.

Impact: Stored XSS via malicious course title.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter or enable autoescape=True globally.

Stored XSS via student name in students.jinja2

The student name is rendered without escaping ({{ name }}) and autoescape is disabled globally. Student names are user-supplied via POST to /students/ and stored in the database, making this a stored XSS vulnerability.

Impact: An attacker can create a student with a malicious name containing JavaScript, which will execute in the browser of any user viewing the students list.

Fix: Apply the |e escape filter: {{ name | e }}, or enable autoescape=True globally.

Missing Authentication on Student Creation (POST /students/)

The students() handler accepts POST requests to create new students without any authentication check. The template hides the form behind `{% if auth_user %}` but the handler itself never verifies `auth_user` or session['user_id']. Any unauthenticated user can POST to /students/ to create students.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can create arbitrary student records in the database.

Fix: Add server-side authentication check at the beginning of the POST branch, e.g., verify session['user_id'] exists or use the @authorize() decorator.

Missing Authorization (Admin Check) on Course Creation (POST /courses/)

Even if authentication were added, the handler does not check that the authenticated user is an admin (is_admin). The template gates the form on `auth_user.is_admin` but the backend has no such check. A non-admin authenticated user could create courses.

Impact: Any authenticated user (not just admins) can create courses by directly POSTing to /courses/.

Fix: Add an authorization check verifying the authenticated user has is_admin=True before allowing course creation.

Cleartext Storage of Database Password

The database password is stored in cleartext in the YAML configuration file. For an application handling PII, credentials should not be stored in plaintext configuration files.

Impact: Anyone with read access to the configuration file (developers, CI/CD systems, container filesystem) can read the database password and access the PostgreSQL database containing sensitive PII.

Fix: Store credentials using environment variables, encrypted secrets, or a dedicated secrets manager. Reference secrets via environment variable interpolation in config files.

Redis Instance Without Authentication

The Redis configuration specifies host, port, and db but no password/authentication. Redis is used for session storage in this application. Without authentication, any entity with network access to the Redis instance can read and manipulate session data.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read session tokens, hijack user sessions, or inject malicious session data. Since the application stores session data in Redis, this could lead to authentication bypass and unauthorized access to PII.

Fix: Configure Redis with a strong password using the 'requirepass' directive and add a 'password' field to the Redis configuration. Also ensure Redis is not exposed outside the Docker network.

PostgreSQL Port Exposed to Host Network

The PostgreSQL database port 5432 is mapped to the host machine's port 5432 via 'ports: - 5432:5432'. Combined with the weak hardcoded credentials (postgres/postgres), this makes the database directly accessible from outside the Docker network, including potentially from the public internet if the host is publicly accessible.

Impact: An attacker on the network can directly connect to the PostgreSQL database using the hardcoded credentials and exfiltrate all PII and application data, or modify/delete data.

Fix: Remove the host port mapping for PostgreSQL. Internal Docker services (like the app container) can communicate with the database via the Docker network without exposing the port to the host. If host access is needed for development, bind only to localhost: '127.0.0.1:5432:5432'.

Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, which allows client-side JavaScript to access the session cookie. Combined with the XSS vulnerabilities from `autoescape=False`, this makes session hijacking trivial.

Impact: If an XSS vulnerability is exploited, attackers can steal session cookies via JavaScript (e.g., `document.cookie`), leading to session hijacking and account takeover.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` in the RedisStorage configuration to prevent JavaScript access to session cookies.

Full User object (including pwd_hash) passed to template context via auth_user_processor

The auth_user_processor context processor passes the full User object (which includes pwd_hash, is_admin, and all other fields) into every template context as 'auth_user'. The User NamedTuple contains sensitive fields: pwd_hash (MD5 password hash), is_admin (privilege flag). This data crosses a trust boundary from server-side into the template rendering context, where it could be rendered or leaked via template debugging, error pages, or future template changes.

Impact: Password hashes and admin status are exposed in the template rendering context for every request. If any template renders the full object (e.g., {{ auth_user }}) or accesses {{ auth_user.pwd_hash }}, the MD5 password hash is disclosed to the user. Even without explicit rendering, the data is available in the template context crossing a trust boundary.

Fix: Instead of passing the full User object, create a safe representation that only includes non-sensitive fields (id, first_name, last_name, username). For example, create a SafeUser NamedTuple or dict that excludes pwd_hash and is_admin, or cherry-pick only the fields needed for display.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint (POST /)

The index() handler processes login POST requests without any rate limiting. An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force password guessing attempts.

Impact: Attackers can brute-force user credentials without any throttling or lockout mechanism.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint, such as limiting attempts per IP or per username, and consider account lockout after repeated failures.

Session Fixation on Login

After successful authentication in the index() handler, the session ID is not regenerated. The handler simply sets session['user_id'] on the existing session. An attacker who knows or sets a victim's session ID before login can hijack the session after the victim authenticates.

Impact: An attacker can fixate a session ID and then hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in.

Fix: Regenerate the session ID after successful authentication by creating a new session and invalidating the old one.

Full Exception Object Passed to Error Template Context (50x) Enabling Information Disclosure

The `handle_50x` error handler passes the full exception object to the 50x template context. The 50x template then renders `{{ error.__dict__ }}`, exposing internal server details. The root cause is passing the full exception object to the template.

Impact: Internal server state, file paths, configuration details, and potentially sensitive data are exposed to end users through the error page.

Fix: Pass only safe, generic error information to the template. Log detailed exception information server-side.

Redis Connection Without Authentication

The Redis configuration schema defines only 'host', 'port', and 'db' fields but has no 'password' field. This means Redis connections are established without authentication. Redis stores session data (including user authentication state), so unauthenticated access to Redis could allow session hijacking.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read and modify session data without authentication, potentially hijacking user sessions or escalating privileges.

Fix: Add a 'password' field to the Redis configuration schema and pass it when creating the Redis connection pool.

Redis connection without authentication

The Redis configuration schema defines only host, port, and db fields but has no password field. This means Redis connections are established without authentication. Redis stores session data (including user IDs and CSRF tokens), and an unauthenticated Redis instance is accessible to anyone who can reach it on the network.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read and modify session data without authentication, potentially hijacking user sessions, escalating privileges, or extracting CSRF tokens.

Fix: Add a 'password' field to the Redis configuration schema and use it when creating the Redis connection pool. Ensure Redis is configured to require authentication.

PostgreSQL Connection Without SSL/TLS

The PostgreSQL DSN is constructed without any SSL/TLS parameters (e.g., `sslmode=require`). Database credentials and all query data (including PII) are transmitted in cleartext over the network.

Impact: An attacker performing network sniffing can intercept database credentials and all PII data transmitted between the application and the database.

Fix: Add `sslmode=require` (or `sslmode=verify-full`) to the DSN string to enforce encrypted connections to PostgreSQL.

Redis Connection Without Authentication (Connection Code)

The Redis connection is established without providing a password parameter. The `aioredis.create_pool` call only passes host, port, and db — no authentication credentials. Since Redis stores session data, this leaves session storage unprotected.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read, modify, or delete session data, enabling session hijacking and privilege escalation.

Fix: Pass a password parameter to `aioredis.create_pool` using a configured Redis password.

Information disclosure via error.__dict__ in 50x template

The 500 error template renders the full internal dictionary of the exception object ({{ error.__dict__ }}), which can expose internal server details, stack traces, file paths, and other sensitive information to end users.

Impact: Attackers can trigger server errors to obtain internal application details, aiding further attacks.

Fix: Remove the {{ error.__dict__ }} expression from the production error template. Show only a generic error message to users.

XSS via last_visited session value in index.jinja2

The `{{ last_visited }}` expression renders a session-stored value without escaping. While the value is typically set by the application as an ISO timestamp, if session data is tampered with (e.g., via Redis manipulation), it could contain malicious HTML/JavaScript.

Impact: XSS if session data is manipulated to contain malicious content.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ last_visited | e }}`, or enable autoescape globally.

Missing Rate Limiting on Student Creation (POST /students/)

The students() handler accepts POST requests to create students without any rate limiting, allowing mass creation of records.

Impact: An attacker can flood the database with student records.

Fix: Add rate limiting to the student creation endpoint.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production Application

The application is initialized with `debug=True`, which enables verbose error messages, stack traces, and other debugging features that can leak sensitive information to attackers in a production environment.

Impact: Attackers can obtain detailed error messages, stack traces, and internal application state that aids in further exploitation.

Fix: Set `debug=False` for production deployments, or use an environment variable to control this setting.
